metadata.title: Why Two Identical Anti-HER2 Drugs Kill Completely Different Tumors
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Nanomedicine candidate 01. Source: cancer-nanomedicine/chapters/05-antibody-drug-conjugates-as-nanoscale-medicines.md
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = membrane-permeable payload / bystander spread / T-DXd; CRIMSON = killed cells / confined payload / T-DM1 failure in HER2-low; GOLD = key mechanism highlight
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: Cut DAR, the five-step dose-loss funnel, linker chemistry details, pneumonitis toxicity, and the DAR-8 failure. Show ONLY: same antibody, one payload stays put, one spreads.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Wonder register (voices/wonder/VOICE.md + MEDHAVY.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the MEDHAVY outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Medhavy.com
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A breast cancer patient's tumor tests HER2-low. One drug is approved for it. The other is not. Both drugs carry the exact same HER2-targeting antibody. So what's actually different?
T-DM1 and T-DXd. Identical antibodies. One opens a new disease category. One does not. The difference cannot be in the targeting — we need to look somewhere else entirely.
Two drugs. Same antibody. One treats HER2-low tumors. The other cannot. Here's the question we need to answer: if the antibody is identical, why does it matter?
The antibody controls exactly one step: which cell gets bound. In a HER2-low tumor, only a few cells carry the antigen. Most do not. The antibody reaches only those few.

The intuitive guess: both drugs underperform equally. Antigen is scarce, so little binds, so little kills. Pick whichever tolerates better. That guess turns out to be wrong — and the reason is in what happens after the antibody binds.

T-DM1 uses a non-cleavable linker. After the antibody binds and the drug enters the cell, the freed payload carries a charged fragment. Here's why that matters: charged means it cannot cross the cell membrane.
So T-DM1 kills only the cell it entered. The HER2-negative cells around it — the majority in a HER2-low tumor — survive untouched. The payload is trapped. The tumor largely isn't.

shot.image_description: A field of tumor cells rendered as colored circles on a cream newsprint ground. A few bright teal circles represent HER2-positive cells. The majority are muted ink-gray circles representing HER2-negative cells. Inside one teal cell, a small crimson dot represents payload that cannot escape. All surrounding gray cells remain intact. Editorial flat-print style, no gradients, no glow. Camera slowly pushes in on the one cell with the trapped crimson dot.

props.headline: So T-DM1 kills only the cell it entered. The HER2-negative cells…

beats.narration_text: T-DXd uses a cleavable linker, and its freed payload is membrane-permeable. Here's where it gets interesting: released inside one HER2-positive cell, it diffuses across the membrane into neighboring cells — including the ones that never expressed HER2.

The antibody is not the whole mechanism. It finds the cell. What happens next depends entirely on whether the payload can cross a membrane. That single physical property — membrane permeability — is what separates these two drugs.

In a HER2-low tumor patch: five HER2-positive cells are all the antibody can reach. T-DM1 kills those five. T-DXd from the same five entry points spreads membrane-permeable payload to roughly forty surrounding cells. Same antibody. Eight times more kills. The bystander effect is the whole story.

Same antibody. One payload stays put — confined by charge. One payload spreads — carried by membrane permeability. That is why T-DXd, not T-DM1, treats HER2-low breast cancer. The antibody targets. The payload determines reach.
